overallIssues.issue: Several Foundation Number points are mapped to broad domain nodes rather than specific canonical sub-nodes, reducing semantic precision.
Some partial mappings omit minor syllabus aspects (e.g. odd/even, whole-number place value, calculator-specific skills).

Financial and enterprise problem-solving points (031-034) are mapped only to the broad NUM-MEAS node because the canonical catalog lacks dedicated nodes for simple interest, profit/loss, exchange rates, appreciation/depreciation, etc. This significantly weakens semantic precision.
Several geometry points rely on overly broad or mismatched nodes for solid-figure names, nets, 2-D representations and circle vocabulary because specific canonical nodes are absent.
Some relation values are recorded as 'narrower' when the mapping is better described as partial or weak, e.g. financial contexts, conversion/travel graphs, and calculator-display interpretation.
Assessment depth is occasionally classified as 'reasoning' for tasks that are primarily contextual application, such as interpreting calculator displays and real-life graphs.

The supplied canonical catalog lacks nodes for several distinct Foundation-tier syllabus statements, forcing many candidate points to be left unmapped or weakly mapped. Missing areas include: metric-Imperial conversion, reading scales, tessellation, bearings, irregular-area grid estimation, mass units, vertical line diagrams, time-series line graphs, hypothesis testing, questionnaire design, and measurement estimation/unit choice.

Some candidate points are mapped to canonical nodes that are semantically mismatched or too broad. Examples include mapping the triangle angle-sum property to triangle types, the exterior-angle theorem to the general Angles and Polygons parent, and questionnaire design to sampling bias.
Several syllabus points combine multiple skills and are therefore represented by several canonical nodes, resulting in partial but acceptable mappings given the granularity of the catalog.

The supplied canonical catalog has coverage gaps for several Foundation-tier statistics discussion and probability topics, including misleading graphs, finding patterns and exceptions, drawing inferences and conclusions, correlation versus causality, qualitative probability terms, relative frequency, experimental probability, comparing experimental and theoretical probabilities, total probability sum to 1, temperature charts and grouped frequency diagrams. These points are correctly flagged as unmapped or weak.

Calculator-specific facilities are not represented in the canonical catalog. Order of operations and brackets nodes do exist, so the candidate's unmapped classification for I-NUM-CAL-01 overstates the gap and should be revised to weak or partial.

Paper allocation is correctly left as not-specified. The specification states that both Unit 1 (non-calculator) and Unit 2 (calculator-allowed) may set questions on any part of the subject content, so no fixed topic-to-paper allocation is supported.

Several candidate points use the non-standard relation value 'broader' when mapping one source statement to multiple narrower canonical nodes. The intended meaning (source is broader than the mapped nodes) is understandable but should be reviewed for consistency with the project's relation vocabulary.

Six supplied points remain unmapped because the supplied canonical catalog lacks dedicated nodes for non-calculator written methods, real-world information interpretation, conversion/travel/implicit graphs, and contextual calculator-display interpretation.

Several mappings are broader or partial and omit specific contexts or properties (fraction arithmetic, repeated proportional change/appreciation/depreciation, inverse proportion, perpendicular gradients, products/powers in expressions, quadratic/non-linear sequences).

The canonical catalog lacks dedicated nodes for several Intermediate-tier geometry basics: nets/plans/elevations/isometric representations, circle vocabulary, specific solid figures (cone, sphere, tetrahedron), triangle angle-sum properties, polygon interior/exterior angle sums, and plans/elevations. This forces weak or unmapped mappings for multiple syllabus points.

Two Intermediate-tier algebra points are unmapped because the catalog has no exact nodes for (a) trial-and-improvement solution of cubic equations and (b) distinguishing equations, formulae and expressions.
Source reference for OFFICIAL-WJEC-3300-G-4.1 is an entire page (p.26) but the mapped canonical nodes cover only a small subset of that page's content.

The canonical node catalog lacks GCSE-specific geometry topics including bearings, loci, tessellation, scale drawings/maps, metric-Imperial conversions, estimation of measurements, reading scales, and dimensional analysis. Several valid syllabus points are therefore unmapped or weakly mapped.

The catalog does not contain nodes for mass units, population density, mpg, semicircles, composite shapes, cross-sectional area or composite solids, leading to acceptable but incomplete mappings for some mensuration and measures points.

The catalog's statistics nodes are oriented towards formal inference (hypothesis testing, sampling methods) and do not align well with the GCSE statistical problem-solving process, questionnaire design, fairness, sample size and reliability requirements.

Several statistical and probability topics in the Higher tier specification are not represented by dedicated canonical nodes, including vertical line diagrams, temperature charts, line graphs for time-series, grouped frequency diagrams, frequency polygons, relative frequency, empirical probability, comparison of experimental and theoretical probabilities, and total probability equals 1.
A few mappings reuse algebra/function or reasoning/modelling nodes for statistical concepts where no dedicated statistical node exists, resulting in weak node fit.

Candidate source page references (p.28, p.29, p.30) do not align with the provided PDF pagination, in which the Higher tier Statistics and Probability content appears on [PAGE 42]-[PAGE 44]; the content itself is nevertheless supported by the specification.
Calculator-specific points are either unmapped or mapped to general operation/order/trigonometry nodes because the canonical catalogue lacks calculator-skill nodes.

Several number-content points are mapped to nodes that are too coarse or omit specific official requirements (reading/writing numbers, ordering across forms, odd/even, prime factorisation, reciprocal, repeated proportional change, inverse proportion, non-calculator written methods).

Some points are labelled 'exact' in the candidate data when the selected nodes do not fully capture the official point (e.g., odd/even, reciprocal, calculator ordering, reading calculator display); relation metadata should be downgraded to partial/acceptable.

Canonical node catalog gaps and weak mappings leave several supported GCSE content areas poorly represented, including rational/irrational numbers, conversion graphs, personal finance, real-life graphs and travel graphs.
Source reference for the rounding-up/down point is incorrect: the content is on p.33, not p.34.
Relation annotation for the personal/household finance point is inconsistent with the mapped node: the point is broader than NUM-MEAS-TIME-MONE, not narrower.
Higher tier graphing content has incomplete or uncertain canonical coverage (implicit graphs, combined functions, asymptotes treated as a property, possible mis-assignment to linear graphs).
Pre-calculus rate-of-change and area-under-graph points are mapped to A-level calculus nodes, overrepresenting formal differentiation/integration in the GCSE specification.
Factorisation and algebraic-fraction points are mapped to equation-solving nodes or left unmapped, and relation metadata does not consistently reflect the semantic mismatch.
Some 'knowledge and use' points are recorded at assessment depth 'knowledge', which may understate the required application.

A significant number of GCSE-specific content items have no corresponding canonical nodes, leaving them unmapped. These include nets, plans and elevations, isometric drawing, 3-D solid vocabulary, angle-sum and exterior-angle theorems for triangles, rational equations reducible to quadratics, cubic trial-and-improvement, and conceptual distinctions between equations, formulae, identities and expressions.

Several mappings are partial or weak: basic geometrical terms omit point/line/plane/circle vocabulary; circle vocabulary is mapped to irrelevant triangle/quadrilateral nodes; construction mappings omit quadrilateral/circle construction and standard-angle construction; polygon angle-sum properties are not captured by the polygon-terms node.
The syllabus point identifiers use inconsistent naming conventions, mixing 'OFFICIAL-WJEC-3300-H-...' and 'WJEC-3300-...' prefixes. This does not affect source support but reduces traceability.

Multiple GCSE Mathematics content items have no matching canonical node, creating coverage gaps: location by distance and angle, tessellation, map interpretation, bearings, loci construction and intersecting loci, metric/Imperial conversions, mass units, volume scale factor for similar 3-D shapes, and measurement estimation.

Transformation mappings are incomplete or semantically mismatched: the combined-transformations point omits translation, and drawing an image under transformation is mapped to a describing-transformations node rather than a drawing/execution node.

Some mappings use acceptable broader nodes but omit sub-skills explicitly in the source, such as any-angle trigonometry, general trigonometric behaviour, parallelogram-vertex coordinates and scale drawings.

The canonical catalog lacks GCSE-specific nodes for several topics, so a number of points are legitimately unmapped. These include scale reading, dimensional analysis of formulae, grid area estimation, systematic sampling, sample-size/reliability effects, vertical line diagrams, time-series line graphs, frequency polygons, grouped frequency diagrams and temperature charts.

Some partial or weak mappings under-represent the breadth of the official point: compound measures (population density), surface area/volume of spheres/cones/compound solids, statistical problem-solving process, GCSE hypothesis work, sampling methods, and stratified sampling.
Candidate identifier scheme is inconsistent: some points use the OFFICIAL-WJEC-3300-H-... prefix while others use WJEC-3300-... This does not affect content but hinders traceability.
Where no canonical node is mapped, the candidate still supplies relation values such as 'exact'. Relation metadata should be omitted or marked as unknown when no mapping exists.
Ten points have empty canonicalNodeIds but declare relation 'exact', which is semantically inconsistent; they should be marked 'unmapped' or 'partial' and documented as catalog gaps.
Four candidate point IDs are non-standard/legacy-style and do not match the official OFFICIAL-WJEC-3300-H-... identifier pattern used elsewhere, suggesting reliance on legacy structured data.

The supplied canonical catalog lacks nodes for several explicit specification concepts, including misleading graphs, relative frequency, experimental probability, total probability, correlation vs causality, and finding patterns/exceptions in data.
Modal category for qualitative data and modal class for grouped data are both mapped to the generic MODE node, which does not distinguish the two specific contexts.

Numerous explicit syllabus statements are recorded only as unmapped (with documented reasons) because the canonical node catalog lacks matching leaves, particularly in calculator use, measures/conversions, constructions/loci, transformations/tessellations, graph types, advanced algebra, and probability. The official content is represented, but ontology coverage gaps limit precision.

Inconsistent identifier conventions across the candidate index (e.g., OFFICIAL-WJEC-3300-FT-/I-/H-/A-/N- prefixes versus bare WJEC-3300-X.X IDs) hinder automated validation and increase the risk of undetected duplicates or merge errors.

The index is scoped to subject content only; assessment objectives (AO1–AO3), entry/resit rules, grading, formula lists and calculator regulations are not represented. If whole-specification completeness is required, these sections should be explicitly scoped out or added.
